text:
Person1: Okay. You read me well enough. Why can't I read you?
Person2: Don't bother
Person1: Dodge ... he's not like me at all. But he makes sense. Held walk naked into a live volcano if he thought he could learn something no other man knew. I understand why  he's here. But you...You're no seeker. You're negative.
Person2: But I'm not prepared to die.
Person1: I'd like to know why not. You thought life on Earth was meaningless. You despised people. So what did you do? You ran away.

Person1: I could see Brad's face before me and my mind screamed No!  But my lips were hungry - too hungry.
Person2: So baby don't cry like there's no tomorrow After the night there's a brand new day. And there'll be no pain And no more sorrow. So wash your face And phone my place It'll be O.K.
Person1: I wanted to be loved completely. My body throbbed excitedly.  Oh Brad, Brad my darling, how could I have done this to you?
Person2: And that's all the time that it takes For a heart to beat again. So give me a sign That a lover makes You look around The one you've found Is back again.
Person1: If only we hadn't made this journey. If only the car hadn't broken down. If only we were amongst friends or sane persons.

Person1: Elton and Stoney know how I feel about you. An' they're just goin' to think I'm not too nice a guy, which I'm not, an' that you're a hell of a person puttin' up with me, that's all.
Person2: You're goin' a find me dead one time.
Person1: Sssh, come on now...  Be a good girl.
Person2: If you really want a get up an' leave me, you can read about it in the newsprint.
Person1: I'm not going to get up an' leave you.  Now let's go over to El's an' have a good time.
Person2: Do you love me, Bobby?

Person1: Magua would use the ways of Les Francais and the Yengeese...
Person2: The red man put down the bow, picked up the fire stick and became the best warrior in the forest. Yes. It is the only way.
Person1: Would the Huron make his Algonquin brothers foolish with brandy and steal his lands to sell them for gold to the white man?  Would the Huron have greed for more land than a man can use? Like Francais Black Robes do?  Would Huron kill tribes with disease? Would the Huron fool Seneca into taking all the animals in the forest for beads & brandy? But sell the fur to the white man for gold?...  Those are the ways of Yengeese and Les Francais masters. Are they the ways of Huron men who hunt & work the land? Or of dogs?  ...Magua's heart is twisted. He would make himself into what twisted him. A Dog, become Master of Dogs. But are Hurons dogs?  ...Magua's way is false. It is like the white sickness. Magua's way will bring only sadness and shame. Is there another way? I don't know.  I am Nathaniel of the Yengeese; Hawkeye, adopted son of Chingachgook, of the Mohican people... Let the children of the dead Munro go free... I speak the truth.

Person1: I couldn't sit home and play the mournful widow anymore, and the police didn't need me, so I tried watching a Lakers game on TV, but the announcer talked to fast and I couldn't understand a lot of what was happening, so I figured if I came down here maybe you could explain the rules to me, and besides, I missed you.
Person2: No problem.

Person1: I'm scared.
Person2: Then why are you sitting here with half the lights off?
Person1: I don't know.
Person2: Well, come on, get your coat. We're going to pick up Paul.
Person1: I don't want to.
Person2: Look, Lindsey, I thought we understood each other...
Person1: I want to stay here and watch this.

Person1: Please... forgive me.
Person2: What?
Person1: Negative thinking makes everything more difficult. If you're going to have enough strength to do this, we have to talk only about positive things. All right?

Person1: I could fight you on this.
Person2: You could.  But, if I leave without your signature, this agreement begins to disintegrate; benefits shrink, options narrow, compensations shrivel.

Person1: Remember, I'm not the one who chose this.
Person2: Yes, so which one of us does that make the coward?

Person1: She coulda killed me last night.
Person2: Too many people coulda seen ya go in her room.  She was a professional. Used to work in the Dutch Schultz gang.
Person1: Who are you?
Person2: Gondorff asked me to look after ya.
Person1: How do I know you're tellin' the truth.
Person2: Don't have much choice, do ya?

Person1: No, I love you, really.
Person2: Then tell me, what will happen? Tell me!
Person1: Look, I love you Katka but I'm twelve years older, I could never marry you, it'd be stupid, you know that!

Person1: I understand Black culture.  I grew up around black people all my life. If the truth be told I probably know "niggers" better than you, Monsieur Delacroix.  Please don't get offended by my use of the quote-unquote N word.  I got a black wife and three bi-racial children, so I feel I have a right to use that word.  I don't give a damn what Spike says, Tarantino is right.  Nigger is just a word.  If Dirty Ole Bastard can use it every other word so can I.
Person2: I would prefer you not use that word in my presence.
Person1: NIGGER.  NIGGER.  NIGGER.  NIGGER.

Person1: Now by killer, you mean...?
Person2: I mean he murdered someone and did time back in Boston. The guy's a freak.
Person1: Jeez, Mary... I'm...
Person2: Well, lucky for me I found out. Thank God I have friends like Tucker.  Look, I'm sick of talking about stalkers.  Let's talk about you.

Person1: You looked just like your father... The first time the shark came to Amity....
Person2: I don't want to talk about that.
Person1: It's a terrible thing to know some- thing no one else wants to believe.
Person2: I'm tired of hearing about it. There's no shark.  Sean wasn't killed by the same shark.. Dad didn't die because of it...It's all wrong... Some fantasy you've concocted...You keep bringing it up.

Person1: Walter...stay away from me!
Person2: Don't you see Carla?  I made them immortal.

Person1: Well, I'm done.  Are you done?
Person2: No --
Person1: Okay, let's go.
Person2: But --

Person1: That's no batlight, it's a birdcall.
Person2: Her name is Pamela Isley.  I saw her talking to Gordon.  She must have stolen his keys, altered the signal-
Person1: And she did it all for me.  For love.
Person2: She's infected us with some kind of pheromone extract-
Person1: Is that it, Bruce?  I'm under some magic spell?
Person2: She wants to kill you.
Person1: You'd say anything to keep me away from her.  To keep her for yourself.
Person2: You once said to me that being part of a team means trusting your partner.  That sometimes counting on someone else is the only way to win.  DO you remember?

Person1: Coach...
Person2: I want you to stand like that until you're sick of it because I don't ever want to see it again, you hear me?  Dora, take her spot.

Person1: You dropped a glass.
Person2: I...
Person1: A cheap wine glass from the caterers.  You started crying.  I tried to comfort you...
Person2: In the living room...
Person1: You shoved me away and ran out to the patio.  I took you home.
Person2: But why?  I can't understand how I could just completely--
Person1: It was maybe a week after the accident. We were moving.  It was a hard time.
Person2: But I don't remember.
Person1: Sweetheart, it was over a year ago.
Person2: What's wrong with me?

Person1: Get out of this room and get back to work.
Person2: I don't have to listen to you anymore.
Person1: I am in charge now. Get back to work or I'll fire you.
Person2: Let me ask you Mr. Manager, do you notice a smell of cockroaches? If I were to leave this room, I'm sure I could find this smell. I know people who could cleanse it. But maybe it doesn't bother you? Why is that? Are used to this smell? Not me, I need a clean room to escape it.

Person1: You don't expect me to believe that for one minute, do you?  Does it matter what's on them? Really? ... Murder, Dick?  Sex?  Your secrets, your fantasies?  Or just me and you and ...
Person2: Don't be ridiculous!
Person1: I remember Alger Hiss.  I know how ugly you can be -- you're capable of anything.  But you see, it doesn't really matter, at the end of the day, what's on them.  Because you have absolutely no remorse.  No concept of remorse.  You want the tapes to get out, you want them to see you at your worst ...
Person2: You're drunk!

Person1: Elaborate on 'they,' okay?
Person2: There are all kinds of groups, all kinds of initials.  But they're all part of two warring factions. One: families that have held wealth for centuries. They want one thing.  Stability. Group Two:  the boat rockers. Eisenhower's military industrial complex.  They want instability. It's a trillion dollar a year business.  When there isn't a hot war, they make a cold one.
Person1: Cold War's over, Jerry.
Person2: So now they feed us terrorists. To create fear.  How much do you think an airport security system goes for?  Then multiply it by every airport in the country.
Person1: And you think Group One is at war with Group Two.
Person2: Latest casualty?  Ernest Harriman. You heard of him?
Person1: Sure. One of the richest men in America until he died a few days ago.
Person2: His obituary was in every paper. But not one of them said he was murdered.
Person1: Murdered?
Person2: Right here in Manhattan.
Person1: It said in the paper he drowned in a swimming pool.  In Newport.
Person2: Nobody dies in Newport.  They couldn't even kill Sunny von Bulow there.  Harriman drowned, but it wasn't in Newport.
Person1: Where then?

Person1: I thought I would accompany you to the airport to say bon voyage... and tell you that Marcel will be going with you.
Person2: What?
Person1: This is a considerable move up for you, Daniel. The temptation of having so much money might be too much for you.
Person2: You don't trust me?
Person1: I don't trust anyone. You don't get to the top of this game by trusting people... and after all, you are a thief. It's in your nature to steal. I'm just protecting my investment.

Person1: Something's wrong with you.
Person2: Nothing is wrong with me.  I know something's in there - and so do you.

Person1: Our friends in Little Russia.  Go in and ask for a man named Dmitri.
Person2: Dmitri?
Person1: The man who hired me for this contract.
Person2: I don't get it.
Person1: You're gonna be me.  You're gonna go in, and you're gonna get the info on the remaining two hits.
Person2: Why me?  Why don't you do it?
Person1: No client has ever seen my face, and I intend to keep it that way.  Besides, if he decides to put a bullet in my head, I don't wanna be there for it.
Person2: He's gonna shoot me?
Person1: When he finds out you tossed his list? I would.
Person2: No.  No way.  I can't do this.
Person1: Max.  You threw my briefcase in the river.  You've got balls bigger than Toledo.
Person2: I...I wasn't thinking.  I just did it.
Person1: That's jazz, my friend.  You said it yourself.  So don't tell me you don't know how to play between the notes.

Person1: You're damn right it's not like that! Because I won't let it be like that! You want your flirt? Fine! The flirt is yours!
Person2: I don't want Caitlin...
Person1: You don't know what you want, but I'm not going to sit here anymore holding your hand until you figure it out! I've encouraged you to get out of this lousy dump and go back to school, to take charge of your life and find direction. I even transferred so maybe you would be more inclined to go back to college if I was with you. Everyone said it was a stupid move, but I didn't care because I loved you and wanted to see you pull yourself out of this senseless funk you've been in since that woman dumped you, oh so many years ago. And now you want to go back to her so she can mess with you some more?
Person2: I don't want to go back with her...
Person1: Of course not; not now! You're caught, and now you're trying to snake out of doing what you wanted to do. Well, I won't let you. I want you to follow through on this, just so you can find out what a ridiculous idiot you are. And when she dumps you again- and she will, Dante, I promise you that-when she dumps you again, I want to laugh at you, right in your face, just so you realize that that was what you gave up our relationship for!  I'm just glad Randal had the balls to tell me, since you couldn't.
Person2: Randal...?
Person1: And having him tell me... that was just the weakest move ever. You're spineless.
Person2: Veronica, I love you...
Person1: Forget you.

Person1: Youre right. I was so desperate to make everyone believe I was happy that I spent six grand on a lie. And after all that? The only one who ended up falling for it was me.
Person2: Go ahead, Kat. Hate me. You were running out of steam on the whole Jeffrey thing. Maybe now youll be able to hold onto this just long enough to ruin your next relationship.

Person1: I'm not asking you anything. I'm telling you what I'm going to do!
Person2: And you think I will permit this, my friend?
Person1: You've got to permit it! Whose fault is it that no one comes here? The museum at Walston Lane does well enough, and why? They've got Jack the Ripper, Burke and Hare, the Mad Butcher, the Demon Barber of Fleet Street and things people pay to see.
Person2: And they are welcome to them. To perpetuate such creatures is to celebrate their crimes.
Person1: Well, what do you think I'm in this for?  Think of it, man, a match -- a cigar stub -- and ten thousand pounds to divide between us!
Person2: You're insane.
Person1: Not at all -- let me show you how easily it can be done.

Person1: That was so money. It was like that "Jedi mind" shit.
Person2: That's what I'm telling you, baby. The babies love that stuff. They don't want all that sensitive shit. You start talking to them about puppy dogs and ice cream. They know what you want. What do you think? You think they don't?
Person1: I know. I know.
Person2: They know what you want, believe me. Pretending is just a waste of time. You're gonna take them there eventually anyway. Don't apologize for it.
Person1: I'm just trying to be a gentleman, show some respect...
Person2: Respect, my ass. They respect honesty. You see how they dress when they go out? They want to be noticed. You're just showing them it's working. You gotta get off this respect kick, baby. There ain't nothing wrong with letting them now that you're money and that you want to party.

Person1: We can at least fight -- we might be able to kill a few--
Person2: Will -- it'll go worse for us -- for Elizabeth, especially -- if we fight.

Person1: Mr. Roston, what was your relationship with Miss Lawson?
Person2: We were lovers.
Person1: How long were you together?
Person2: For about one year.
Person1: How would you describe your sex life with Miss Lawson?
Person2: Intense.
Person1: I know this is a very personal subject, but could you be a little more specific?
Person2: It was wild.  She was constantly trying to get me more and more worked up -- kinky things.  I tried to satisfy her the best I could, but it was difficult in my condition.
Person1: What kind of condition are you referring to?
Person2: I had a bad heart.

Person1: Rick, I want to talk to you.
Person2: Ah, Cole.  I don't remember ordering an asshole from room service.

Person1: Why do you feel the need to explore this side of your personality?
Person2: What are you talking about?
Person1: I'm talking about the kind of people you hang out with...about growing up, assuming responsibility of yourself.
Person2: I asked you to marry me. I'm ready for marriage. That's responsibility. That's growth.
Person1: I just think that at some point you're going to have to re-evaluate some of your friendships...
Person2: Who else?
Person1: Charles Moore for instants.
Person2: You don't like Moore? Since when?
Person1: It's not that I don't like him. But the wedding has really got me thinking and... I just keep myself opening up. Crowning. And I want you keeping up with me here.
Person2: What does Moore have to do with your growing?
Person1: I just don't see him in the big picture.
Person2: I've known him since Cub Scouts.
Person1: He's weird.
Person2: He's quiet.
Person1: He's weird.

Person1: I don't know what it is, but there is something happening here, and it's starting to scare the living shit out me, and look, I'm just not going to argue the point anymore--you want to stay here, stay, but I've got to get out of here, and I'm begging you to come with me.
Person2: I can't.
Person1: Then I'm going--
Person2: --no, you're not. You love me too much. You don't want to see them kill me.

Person1: You wanted to become a member of the most powerful clique in the school. If I wasn't already the head of it, I'd want the same thing.
Person2: I'm sorry? What are you oozing about?
Person1: That episode with the note back there was for all of us to enjoy, but you're determined to ruin my day.
Person2: We made a girl want to consider suicide. What a scream. What a jest.
Person1: Come on you jerk. You know you used to have a sense of humor.

Person1: Get lost, you jerk.
Person2: It would be a pity to squander such a strong personality.

Person1: We're gonna die!
Person1: We're gonna die!
Person2: Hey, hey, hey.
Person1: ...
Person2: Put your hand in my pants.
Person1: What?
Person2: ...
Person1: We're gonna die, and you want me to do some freaky shit like that?!
Person2: Arlo!  Arlo.
Person2: Reach into my pocket.
Person1: ...
Person2: Come on!
Person1: Oh.
Person1: Okay.
Person2: Yeah.
Person1: Okay.
Person2: ...
Person1: Okay.  All right, man.  Okay.
Person1: ...
Person2: Okay.
Person1: All right.  Okay.
Person2: ...
Person1: Okay.
Person2: ...
Person2: Arlo!
Person2: ...
Person1: I'm sorry.  I'm a little

Person1: What the hell is the President trying to do?  Steal DeHaven's thunder?
Person2: I think it's more important, sir, to decide what we're going to do -- since it's apparent this issue is not going away quietly.
Person1: "G.I. Jane."  And which one of you told me she wouldn't last a week? Huh?

Person1: I suppose you don't think that's funny?
Person2: No.
Person1: It seemed funny to me when I first heard it. Maybe the trouble isn't with the joke. Maybe it's with you!
Person2: I don't think so.
Person1: Maybe you haven't any sense of humor. Well, I'll give you one more chance! Now listen!

Person1: ...'Shnook'? 'Dope'? 'Dipstick'? 'Lamebrain'?
Person2: No, sir.
Person1: Not even behind your back?
Person2: Sir! They voted me most likely to succeed!
Person1: You're fired.
Person2: But, sir! --
Person1: Get your feet off that desk.

Person1: Come off it, Jack! Of course you can check to see if she's been arrested.
Person2: I'm sorry, Sam, I'm afraid this whole case has become much more complicated since last we talked.
Person1: She's innocent, Jack --- she's done nothing wrong.
Person2: Tell that to the wives of the Security men she blew up this afternoon. Listen, we've also had a report just in from Central Services that Tuttle has wrecked an entire flat and sabotaged adjacent Central Services systems - as a matter of fact, in your block. I'd keep my eyes open if I were you, Sam. Bye.
Person1: You don't really think Tuttle and the girl are in league?
Person2: I do. Goodbye.

Person1: All right, look.  I can make it Right, I can make this up to you, Mattie.  I can, I swear.
Person2: Will you go to her and tell her Right in front of me she's Nothing to you?  Right out loud so I can hear?  Tell her she's Nothing, tell her she's nobody, Just dirt?  Will you do that?

Person1: I love you.
Person2: Right.  That's why you tried to kill me.
Person1: Nooo!  I would never do anything to hurt you.  I want you to come back to me.
Person2: Bullshit.

Person1: But I know you're not gonna quit on me.
Person2: How do you know that?
Person1: Cause you got a little voice in your head saying, do the right thing.  Give him another chance.

Person1: -- But I wanted you to know first who it was who had beaten you. I, Khan Noonian Singh, the eagle you attempted to cage forever.
Person2: Khan, listen to me -- if its me you want, I'll have myself beamed aboard. All I ask is that you spare my crew.
Person1: That is a most intriguing offer. Typical, I must say of your sterling character. Let me think.

Person1: They had it at the store, Paul, there was a whole batch of them there. As soon as I saw it, I slammed my money down. I got the first copy.
Person2: Then the roads are open...
Person1: The one to town is, but that's about it. I called the hospital and talked to the head orthopedic surgeon. I told him who you were and what had happened. He said as long as there's no infection, you're not in any danger, and as soon as the road to the hospital is open, they'll send an ambulance for you.
Person2: The phones are working?
Person1: Well, mine's still out. But the ones in town were working just fine. I called that agent of yours.  Oh, Paul, I peeked at the very beginning.  What a wonderful first page--just to read the name Misery Chastain...
Person2: My daughter must be going nuts.
Person1: ...it's like a visit from my oldest, dearest friend.
Person2: I was supposed to be home for her birthday three days ago.
Person1: Your agent said she would tell her you were okay. But I'm afraid you'll have to wait until tomorrow if you want to speak to her yourself.

Person1: A layman..., layman. Interesting term. So many experts on government grants. And you say you're a 'layman?' But it was you who caught me, wasn't it, Will? Do you know how you did it'
Person2: You've read the transcript. It's all there.
Person1: No it's not. Do you know how you did it Will?
Person2: It's in the transcript. What does it matter now?
Person1: It doesn't matter to me, Will.
Person2: I want you to help me, Dr. Lecktor.
Person1: Yes, I thought so.
Person2: It's about Atlanta and Birmingham.
Person1: Yes.
Person2: You read about it, I'm sure.
Person1: In the papers. I don't tear out the articles.  I wouldn't want them to think I was dwelling on anything morbid. You want to know how he's choosing them, don't you?
Person2: I thought you would have some ideas.
Person1: Why should I tell you?
Person2: There are things you don't have. Research materials... I could speak to the Chief of Staff...?
Person1: Chilton? Gruesome, isn't he? He fumbles at your head like a freshman pulling at a panty girdle.  He actually tries to give me a Thematic and Apperception test. Hah. Sat there waiting for MF-13 to come up. It's a card with a woman in bed and a man in the foreground.  I was supposed to avoid a sexual interpretation. I laughed in his face.  Never mind, it's boring.
Person2: You'll get to see the file on this case. And there's another reason.
Person1: Pray tell.
Person2: I thought you might be curious to find our if you're smarter than the person I'm looking for.
Person1: Then by implication, you think that you are smarter than me, since you caught me.
Person2: No. I knew that I'm not smarter than you are.
Person1: Then how did you catch me, Will?
Person2: You had disadvantages.
Person1: What disadvantage?
Person2: You're insane.
Person1: You're very tan, Will.

Person1: I want out.  I want a new life.  I see what I've been waiting for, but I need you, Corky.
Person2: For what?
Person1: You made a choice once.  Do you think you would make that same choice again?
Person2: What choice?
Person1: If those quarters fell to the floor, would you still reach up to that cash register?

Person1: We haven't got much time left, Mr. Dickson. We've got to do something quick or it'll be too late.
Person2: Why wouldn't you tell me where you were last night?
Person1: You're not giving up, are you, Mr. Dickson?
Person2: Were you in Cluett's apartment?
Person1: Oh, I can explain about that later. You're losing your bank - don't you realize what that means?
Person2: Was Mrs. Dickson there?
Person1: Listen, Mr. Dickson, don't let them lick you just because a couple of big shots turned you down. You've got more friends than anybody in this town. Little guys - guys who wouldn't be in business if it weren't for you. All you've got to do is
Person2: Wait a minute. Answer my question. Was Mrs. Dickson there?
Person1: Well . . . uh . . . I . . .
Person2: She was, wasn't she? How long has this been going on? Do you know?
Person1: Aw, I don't know what you're talking about. All I know is that you're losing your bank and
Person2: All right. That's all.  Please, Matt.

Person1: You have a beautiful face... the way the light hits it.  I'd like to take your picture.  You don't mind?
Person2: I'd rather you didn't.
Person1: What's the problem?
Person2: I'm camera shy.
Person1: You trust me to keep your money, but not to take your picture?
Person2: Those are two different kinds of trust.  Thank you for your time.  I hope we can do business.

Person1: Is he leaving right this minute?
Person2: We've got some paperwork to take care of. Pretty soon. Don't worry, you won't run into him in the parking lot.
Person1: I didn't mean that.
Person2: I hope the best for you, Miss Dwiggins, with your school and your paper and all.
Person1: Where will he go?
Person2: Wherever he wants to. I think he's going back to Millsburg where he's from. It's just about twenty miles from here.
Person1: Will he be supervised?
Person2: As much as anybody else is, I guess. Y'all have a good rest of the day now.

Person1: No.
Person2: Okay.
Person1: Pretty much a disaster, tonight, wasn't it?
Person2: I guess.
Person1: Life gets messy.  Ugly messy.  But I don't understand you.  And I don't think I ever understood Lucy.  I don't understand throwing it away.  How do you throw all that away?  Any of it.  I want it all.  You guys want guarantees?  I want the possibilities.  And all kinds of crap comes with that.  A lot of bad shit. And I think that's okay with me because, because of the rest of the stuff.  All the good shit.  All the surprises.  It's a miracle when you come down to it.  We'd have had amazing children, you and me.  We'd have had a ride.  You'd have surprised yourself.  I'll never love anybody else, you know.
Person2: Me too.
Person1: That's under lock and key.
Person2: Me too.

Person1: Easy, hon, I was just looking for fun details --
Person2: Oh, well, why didn't you say so? And oh, I don't know if you're interested in this detail, but I was just about to tell you that I love him.  I love him, and I don't care what you think.  I love him for the guy he wants to be, and I love him for the guy he almost is. I love him.

Person1: This baseball player you're going out with...  He's no good for you.
Person2: Really?! He's a wonderful guy. He makes two million a year, and he worships me.
Person1: I worship you.
Person2: You worship yourself.
Person1: Ronnie, forget this what's-his-name.
Person2: Greg.
Person1: Did you know he's already got a bad knee? In another 10 years you're going to be pushing him around in a wheelchair.

Person1: I overheard you telling Mark that I wasn't qualified for the promotion.
Person2: That's not what I said. I mentioned that you were newer to the team.
Person1: So you're implying I don't have the experience.
Person2: You are making that up. I never said you weren't qualified. I was just discussing team dynamics.

Person1: I'm sorry but I have to leave. I'll find other work. You'll have to get someone else, that's all.
Person2: What is this, Emmi?
Person1: I can't do this. It's not right.
Person2: What's not right?
Person1: I have been questioned by the Gestapo just like that. Just like you questioned him.
Person2: Emmi, stop! I want to show you something. Let me show you something and then if you want to leave, you can leave, please please. His friends, they did this. And he gave them birthday concerts.
Person1: But he had no idea, a lot of people had no idea. I only realised what was really going on when I got arrested.

Person1: Come on, admit it. K worried you, didn't he? He always worried you. In 1942, he's thirty-four years old, you're already fifty-six. Never mind art and politics and symbols and airy-fairy bullshit about liberty, humanity and justice because I don't care how great you are. It's the oldest story in the book,  The ageing Romeo jealous of the young buck. The real reason you didn't leave the country when you knew you should have was that you were frightened that, once you were out of the way, you'd be supplanted by the Miracle Kid, the Party's boy twice over, flashy, talented little K.

But, there is one guy who doesn't like little K as much as he likes you - yeah, the number one man your old pal, Adolf. He thinks you're the greatest, and when he says, I want Wilhelm for my birthday, boy, they better go out get Wilhelm. So, Josef calls and threatens you with little K. And you said to hell with the Ninth in Vienna, I'll give it to Adolf as a birthday present in Berlin. That's the trick they played, they got you by the balls and they squeezed. Hard. Why did you stay? Why did you play for them? Why were you the flag-carrier for their regime?  Jealousy?

You sure were right about one thing, Deborah, the job was too formidable for me. I should have thought a little more before I left for Los Angeles. But there I was focused so hard on Friedman Id stopped thinking.
